original_abstract:
This volume examines the critical paradox of contemporary education: how systemic efforts toward inclusion frequently generate new and more refined forms of exclusion. By drawing on a diverse range of international perspectives—including case studies from Australia, Europe, Africa, and Asia—the contributors analyze the "exclusionary pressures" that undermine equitable schooling. The book interrogates the role of neoliberal management, the labeling of special educational needs, and the impact of rigid behavioral policies on marginalized groups.

Across three thematic sections, the collection explores how institutional structures and policy frameworks disadvantage specific populations, including Indigenous communities, refugees, Roma children, and those affected by gender-based violence. By revisiting the theoretical foundations of inclusive education and scrutinizing the practical realities of segregation and suspicion within school systems, the authors highlight the "wicked problems" that prevent genuine belonging. Ultimately, this work offers a rigorous critique of the global education landscape, identifying the mechanisms through which inclusionary rhetoric can mask exclusionary practices.
humanized_abstract:
Schooling and Society examines the paradox of contemporary schooling where seemingly rational moves aimed at achieving greater social, cultural and economic equality through the mechanisms of schooling increasingly produce finer grain and ever more complex forms of exclusion. Drawing on the perspectives of scholars from around the world — spanning countries as diverse as Australia, Europe, Africa and Asia — and focusing on exclusion pressures that stymie more inclusive forms of schooling, this volume critically investigates the ways in which neoliberal regimes of educational governance; policies and practices around the labelling of children with special educational needs; and various forms of behavioural control are all implicated in exclusionary effects for particular social groups.

The volume as a whole delves into three main themes which address how institutional and policy barriers disadvantage a variety of disadvantaged groups such as Indigenous people, refugees, Roma children and those affected by gender-based violence. Through an analysis of theories of inclusive education and the empirical mechanisms of exclusion through segregation and suspicion in schools, the volume delves into what is meant by 'wicked problems' and how such problems impede school integration and inclusive education leading to non-belonging. This volume therefore offers a critical look at the state of education on a global scale, unveiling the mechanisms that allow the rhetoric of inclusion to veil the practice of exclusion.
